turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg has our opinion in case 16-149, Coventry Health Care of Missouri versus Nevils.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: This case concerns whether Federal Law overrides state law in the context of health insurance plans for federal employees. The Federal Employees Health Benefits Act, acronym FEHBA, authorizes the federal agency, the Office of Personnel Management, OPM, to contract with private insurance carriers to provide health insurance to federal employees. FEHBA contains an express preemption provision which states the terms of any contract concerning health insurance, coverage of benefits including payments with respective benefits shall supersede and preempt any state or local law regarding health insurance. OPM's contracts with private carriers provide for reimbursement and subrogation. Reimbursement means, an insured employee who receives payment from another source for example, the proceeds of a personnel injury law suit must pay back medical expense outlays previously made by the insurance carrier. Subrogation involves transfer of the right to a third-party payment from the insured employee to the carrier, so the carrier in place of the injured employee can pursue the claim against the third party. Respondent in this case, Jodie Nevils was a federal employee insured under a FEHBA plan offered by petitioner, Coventry Healthcare Care of Missouri. When Nevils was injured in a car accident, Coventry paid his medical expenses. Nevils then sued the car driver who caused his injuries and recovered a settlement award. Coventry sought and obtained reimbursement from Nevils for the medical expenses the insurer had paid out for Nevils' care. Nevils then filed a class action in Missouri State Court alleging that Missouri law does not allow insurers to enforce reimbursement provisions. Coventry countered that FEHBA overrode Missouri's anti-reimbursement law. The Trial Court ruled in Coventry's favor and the Missouri Court of Appeals affirmed. The Missouri Supreme Court however reversed. That Court invoked a presumption against preemption to conclude that the federal statute did not override state law under which subrogation and reimbursement clauses were unenforceable. We reverse the Missouri Supreme Court's judgment and hold that FEHBA's preemption clause overrides state law barring subrogation and reimbursement. Contractual subrogation and reimbursement prescriptions relate to payments with respect to benefits. The carrier's very provision of benefits is what triggers the right to reimbursement. FEHBA concerns federal health Insurance benefits provided for federal employees pursuant to federal law. Distinctly, federal interests are served by uniformed administration of the plans free from state interference. Subrogation and reimbursement yield cost savings lowering premiums for the federal government and the enrollees. The FEHBA regime is entirely compatible with the Supremacy Clause, the federal statute itself, not the private contract OPM negotiates pursuant to the statute, strips the state law of its force. For these reasons and others set out in the opinion, the judgment of the Missouri Supreme Court is reversed, and case is remanded for further proceedings not inconsistent with this opinion. All participating justices joined the Court's opinion. Justice Thomas has in addition, filed a concurring opinion. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
